raw_case:
G2 neuroendocrine tumors of the larynx: A case report


Introduction

Laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasms (LNENs) are rare and highly heterogeneous malignant tumors.

Case presentation

We report a case of a patient who underwent multiple surgeries before being diagnosed as G2 Neuroendocrine Tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx, and we hope that our experience can increase surgeons' understanding of LNENs.

Discussion

G2 Neuroendocrine Tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx is a type of LNEN that is red and lumpy and is occasionally misdiagnosed as a hemangioma.

Conclusion

The treatment for G2 NET of the larynx is partial laryngectomy and neck lymph node dissection, local resection is easy to relapse, and the diagnosis depends on immunohistochemistry.

Highlights

•Laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasms (LNENs) are rare and highly heterogeneous malignant tumors.•A G2 NET of the larynx is a type of LNEN that is red and lumpy and is occasionally misdiagnosed as a hemangioma.•The recommended treatment for atypical carcinoid tumors is radical surgical resection with cervical lymph node dissection.•Regardless of adequate treatment, most patients will experience recurrence (62.5%) after 5 years of follow-up.1

Introduction

Neuroendocrine neoplasms (NENs) are rare and highly heterogeneous malignant tumors that originate from neuroendocrine cells. Their most common locations are the lungs, pancreas, rectum, appendix, and small intestine. They are rare in head and neck region. Laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasms (LNENs) account for less than 1 % of all laryngeal tumors, and the majority of them occur in the supraglottic region [1].The symptoms of LNEN, such as pharyngeal paresthesia, hoarseness and dysphagia, vary depending on the location and size of the tumors. Their treatment and prognoses depend on the specific pathological type; therefore, different classifications have been developed. While many pathological types of laryngeal neuroendocrine tumors have been proposed in the past, the previously accepted classifications are typical carcinoid, atypical carcinoid, small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma. As LNENs are being better understood, their classification has evolved. According to the 2022 WHO classification of Head and Neck Tumors, epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms are subtyped as well-differentiated and poorly differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms; well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms are termed neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) and are graded as G1 NETs, G2 NETs and G3 NETs. Poorly differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms are termed neuroendocrine carcinomas and are further classified into small-cell and large-cell neuroendocrine carcinomas [2].G1 NETs and G2 NETs correspond to typical carcinoid and atypical carcinoid tumors respectively.A G2 NET of the larynx is a type of LNEN that is red and lumpy and is occasionally misdiagnosed as a hemangioma. We report the case of a patient who underwent several surgeries before being diagnosed as a G2 NET. We introduce the diagnosis and treatment of a patient with a G2 NET of the larynx, and we hope that our experience can increase surgeons' understanding of LNEN.Our work has been reported in line with the SCARE Guidelines 2023 criteria [3].2

Case presentation

A 67-year-old man presented to our Otolaryngology Department after experiencing pharyngeal paresthesia for 3 years. He had smoked 20 cigarettes a day for approximately 50 years but stopped smoking approximately 1 year ago. He had undergone two surgeries on his larynx. The first operation was performed at another hospital in June 2021. The results of the histopathological examination were unknown, but the electronic laryngoscopy results were similar to those of a laryngeal hemangioma (Fig. 1A). The second operation was performed at our hospital in October 2022, and preoperative electronic laryngoscopy revealed a light pink, lobular mass on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold (Fig. 1B). Biopsy revealed atypical cell hyperplasia, considering the patient's preference and the existing examination results, we performed local excision alone. Postoperative pathology revealed a laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasm, the specific pathological type was uncertain, and immunohistochemistry was recommended for this patient, but the patient did not agree for economic reasons. We returned to this admission, the rest of his medical, surgical, family, and social history were noncontributory. Blood counts, liver and kidney function were within normal limits. Electronic laryngoscopy revealed a spherical, bright red mass located on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold (Fig. 1C). There was no obvious abnormality in the lung computed tomography. Cervical magnetic resonance revealed a spherical mass on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold and multiple lymph nodes on both sides of the jaw and neck. Contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance of the neck revealed no enhancement in the mass, but the right aryepiglottic fold was mildly enhanced (Fig. 2). On the basis of the above examinations and his previous surgical history, a clinical diagnosis of laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasm was made initially.Then, we performed partial laryngectomy and neck lymph node dissection for this patient. No malignancies found in the safety margin during the operation. Pathology and immunohistochemistry revealed the tumor to be a well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine tumor, Grade 2. No cervical lymph node metastasis. Consequently, the diagnosis was modified to a G2 neuroendocrine tumor of the larynx. The patient was discharged from the hospital after the operation and was followed up regularly. At the 3-month postoperative follow-up, we performed electronic laryngoscopy to evaluate the patient's condition and found no signs of recurrence. At the 1-year follow-up after the operation, the patient's condition was good, and there was no sign of recurrence.3

Discussion

Laryngeal G2 NET lesions are macroscopically present as dark red polypoid or nodular masses located on the laryngeal surface of the epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold, and the surface is still smooth, which is very different from the cauliflower-like or ulcer-like growth pattern of common malignant tumors such as laryngeal cancer, so they are easily misdiagnosed as benign tumors such as hemangiomas.The disease is mainly diagnosed via a biopsy with immunohistochemical studies and electron microscopic investigations. Specific pathologic identification is highly important for determining the correct treatment and prognosis. However, it is difficult to make a definite diagnosis via only light microscopy. In this case, the patient underwent a biopsy before the second operation, but the pathologic examination revealed atypical cell hyperplasia, and a definite diagnosis was not reached. This may be related to the superficiality of the pathological materials used and the small sample size. Considering the patient's preference and the existing examination results, we performed local excision alone. Postoperative pathological examination revealed a laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasm, and immunohistochemistry was recommended to clarify the classification. The patient refused immunohistochemistry for economic reasons. It was not until after the third operation that immunohistochemistry was performed to clarify the classification. The limitation of this case was that the pathology was unknown after the first operation, and the patient did not undergo immunohistochemistry after the second operation. If the pathology after the first operation is known, the second operation may be different. If the patient had immunohistochemistry after the second operation, we might have intervened earlier. To some extent, these can lead to recurrence.Laryngeal atypical carcinoids are highly invasive and prone to metastasis. At present, no specific and comprehensive treatment protocol exists for laryngeal atypical carcinoids [4]. The recommended treatment for atypical carcinoid tumors is radical surgical resection with cervical lymph node dissection. Selective neck dissection should be performed even in patients with N0 tumors because of the high incidence of both early cervical metastasis and subsequent involvement of the cervical lymph nodes [5]. In this case, although the specific pathological type was not clear, according to the second operation, the pathology was a neuroendocrine neoplasm. We considered the local recurrence of NETs and the existing examination results; thus, partial laryngectomy with cervical lymph node dissection was performed. Postoperative radiotherapy and chemotherapy were not performed. Because atypical carcinoids are not responsive to chemotherapy, the role of radiotherapy is controversial. Tom P. van der Laan et al. conducted a meta-analysis of 163 reports of atypical laryngeal carcinoids and revealed that, compared with patients who underwent surgery, patients who received initial radiotherapy had lower disease-specific survival (DSS) rates, whereas patients who received postoperative radiotherapy had worse DSS [6]. However, a multicenter study from the United States has shown that radiotherapy combined with other methods is valuable for treating locally advanced or metastatic AC of the lung [7].Regardless of adequate treatment, most patients will experience recurrence (62.5 %) after 5 years of follow-up, and many of them will have distant metastasis (69.4 %). Extending the follow-up period to 10 years revealed a further decrease in disease-specific survival (DSS) compared with the 5-year DSS. Notably, recurrence may occur after the traditional 5-year follow-up period [6]. Therefore, long-term follow-up (10 years or more) is strongly advised for these patients [8].4

Conclusion

The recommended treatment for G2 Neuroendocrine Tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx is partial laryngectomy and neck lymph node dissection, local resection is easy to relapse. The diagnosis depends on immunohistochemistry; it is difficult to make a definite diagnosis via only light microscopy.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Ethical approval for this study is exempt in my institution.

Guarantor

Lin Xiao

Research registration number

None.

Funding

There are no funding sources.

Author contribution

Lin Xiao: provide the case informationTianming Yang: writing the paperHuijun Ren: paper polishing

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 67-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Pharyngeal paresthesia for 3 years.
- **History of Present Illness:** Smoked 20 cigarettes/day for 50 years; quit 1 year ago. Underwent two previous laryngeal surgeries, with biopsy after the second surgery suggesting atypical cell hyperplasia. No signs of malignancy were confirmed until immunohistochemistry during the third surgery.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Smoker for 50 years, ceased smoking 1 year prior.
- **Family History:** Noncontributory.
- **Physical Examination:** Lesion located at the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Normal blood counts, liver, and kidney function.
  - Electronic laryngoscopy: Bright red, spherical lobular mass. 
  - Cervical MRI: Spherical mass on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold; multiple lymph nodes on both sides of the neck.
  - Histopathology and immunohistochemistry post-third surgery: Grade 2 well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **First Step:** Initial diagnosis suggested a benign condition, likely hemangioma, based on electronic laryngoscopy findings (red lobular lesions) and superficial pathological sampling that showed atypical cell hyperplasia. However, the persistence and recurrence of the tumor after local excisions warranted further evaluation.
- **Second Step:** Biopsy results suggestive of a neuroendocrine neoplasm were obtained after the second operation, but the specific pathological type could not be confirmed without immunohistochemistry. Economic constraints delayed further classification and led to recurrence.
- **Third Step:** Immunohistochemistry performed during the third surgical intervention confirmed the tumor as a well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx. The absence of nodal metastasis was verified through cervical lymph node dissection.
- **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:** Hemangioma was excluded based on recurrence, lack of expected vascular features, and histopathological findings. Common malignant laryngeal tumors (e.g., squamous cell carcinoma) were ruled out due to the distinct smooth, red polypoid/nodular gross appearance and lack of surface ulceration.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Grade 2 neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: G2 neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx.

results.content:
### Resoning:

**Step 1: Understanding the Patient's Symptoms and History**

The patient is a 67-year-old male who has been experiencing pharyngeal paresthesia for the past three years. Paresthesia refers to abnormal sensations, such as tingling or numbness, which in this context likely affects the throat area. The patient has a significant smoking history, having smoked 20 cigarettes per day for 50 years, but quit smoking one year ago. This smoking history is crucial because tobacco use is a major risk factor for various head and neck cancers, including those affecting the larynx.

**Step 2: Previous Surgeries and Biopsy Results**

The patient has undergone two previous laryngeal surgeries, with the biopsy after the second surgery indicating atypical cell hyperplasia. Atypical hyperplasia suggests abnormal cell growth, which can be a precursor to cancer. However, no malignancy was confirmed until immunohistochemistry was performed during the third surgery. This indicates that earlier biopsies may not have provided conclusive evidence of cancer, but the persistence of symptoms and the history of smoking warrant further investigation.

**Step 3: Physical Examination and Ancillary Tests**

During the physical examination, a lesion was found on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold. The aryepiglottic fold is a part of the larynx, and lesions in this area can cause symptoms like paresthesia. Ancillary tests include normal blood counts, liver, and kidney function, which suggests that there is no systemic disease affecting these organs at present.

Electronic laryngoscopy revealed a bright red, spherical lobular mass, which is visually concerning and suggests a neoplastic process. Cervical MRI showed a spherical mass on the medial wall of the right aryepiglottic fold and multiple lymph nodes on both sides of the neck. The presence of lymph nodes could indicate local spread or metastasis, depending on their characteristics.

**Step 4: Histopathology and Immunohistochemistry Findings**

The most crucial information comes from the histopathology and immunohistochemistry performed after the third surgery, which diagnosed a grade 2 well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET). Neuroendocrine tumors are a type of cancer that originates from neuroendocrine cells, which are found throughout the body, including the respiratory tract.

**Step 5: Understanding Neuroendocrine Tumors in the Larynx**

Neuroendocrine tumors of the larynx are rare compared to squamous cell carcinomas. They can be classified based on their differentiation and proliferation rates. Grade 2 neuroendocrine tumors are considered to have a higher grade than grade 1 but lower than grade 3, indicating a balance between differentiation and aggressiveness.

**Step 6: Considering Differential Diagnosis**

Given the patient's history of smoking and previous surgeries with atypical hyperplasia, it's essential to consider other potential diagnoses, such as squamous cell carcinoma, which is more common in smokers and can affect the larynx. However, the specific histopathological diagnosis of a neuroendocrine tumor guides the final conclusion.

**Step 7: Integrating All Information for Diagnosis**

Combining the patient's symptoms, history, physical examination, imaging, and pathological findings, the diagnosis is clear. The persistent pharyngeal paresthesia, the location of the lesion in the aryepiglottic fold, the appearance of the mass, and the pathological confirmation all point to a neuroendocrine tumor of the larynx.

**Step 8: Considering Prognosis and Management**

While the diagnosis is established, it's also important to consider the prognosis and management options for this type of tumor. Grade 2 NETs have intermediate characteristics and may require a multidisciplinary approach to treatment, potentially including surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, depending on the extent of disease and lymph node involvement.

**Final Step: Confirming the Diagnosis**

Given all the above considerations, the final diagnosis is a grade 2 well-differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine tumor (G2 NET) of the larynx.

### Answer:

Grade 2 Well-Differentiated Epithelial Neuroendocrine Tumor (G2 NET) of the Larynx
